title: Website Design for Tradies
meta_title: Website Design for Tradies | From $750 | OptiTide
meta_description: A website for tradies, built so someone on a phone can call you or send a quote request. Working Perth hours, Australia-wide. Starter $750, GST inclusive.
intro: Someone with a dead hot water system reaches for their phone, searches, and rings a name near the top of the results. A good website is how you end up being one of those names.
body_html:
What a tradie website actually needs to do

A website for tradies does not need to win design awards. It needs to do three things: show up when someone searches for your trade in your area, prove you know what you are doing, and make it easy to contact you without thinking about it.

Everything else is decoration. Pages nobody reads still cost money to build and time to keep current. What matters is whether the bloke standing in his kitchen with a dead hot water system finds you and taps the call button.

Assume every visitor is on a phone

A tradie search tends to start away from a desk: on a phone, in a hurry, standing next to whatever has stopped working. Build for that visitor and the rest follows.

  • Make the phone number tappable and put it near the top of every page. Not buried in a footer, and not sitting inside an image, where a phone cannot dial it at all.
  • Keep the page light enough to load on mobile data. Photos straight off a camera are heavy, and a page that is still loading is a page that has not made its case yet.
  • Say what you do and where you do it in the first line. "Residential electrician servicing the northern suburbs" beats "Quality workmanship since day one."
  • Keep the quote form short. Name, phone, suburb, what the job is. Every extra field is another reason to give up halfway through.

A quote form is worth having even if you would rather take calls. Someone who will not ring a stranger will happily type three lines at nine at night, and that is a job you would otherwise never hear about.

Showing your work is the whole sales pitch

Trade work has an advantage: the proof is visual. A photo of a properly dressed switchboard, or a retaining wall sitting dead level, argues for you in a way the words "reliable" and "professional" cannot, because every competitor uses those words too.

Take photos as you go. Before and after pairs work especially well. Add a line or two about what the job involved and roughly where it was, because that quietly tells both search engines and customers which areas you cover.

If you hold licences relevant to your trade, list your licence number. A customer who checks it is a customer taking you seriously, and it separates you from anyone who cannot produce one.

Turning up for "electrician near me"

Searches like "electrician near me" or "plumber Joondalup" are the ones worth having, because the person typing them has a problem now rather than a project for one day. A few things move the needle:

  • A page for each service you want more of. If switchboard upgrades are your bread and butter, they deserve their own page rather than one line on a list.
  • Pages for the suburbs and areas you genuinely service. Written properly, with real detail about the work you do there.
  • A free Google Business Profile, filled out completely, with the business name and service area matching your site exactly.
  • Consistent details everywhere. A business name or number that differs across listings muddies the picture for anyone trying to confirm you are the same outfit.

This is ongoing work rather than something you finish. If you want it driven month to month, that is our SEO service, which runs at $750, $1,500 or $2,500 a month.

Why a Facebook page on its own falls short

Running a trade entirely off a Facebook page does work up to a point. Word of mouth spreads there and it costs nothing to set up. The limits are structural rather than a matter of how hard you work at it.

A Facebook page is not built to be found through a Google search the way a website is. Posts move down the feed as newer ones arrive, so a job you posted last month sits somewhere nobody scrolls to. You do not control the layout, what appears alongside your work, or what the platform changes next year. And there is no quote form landing in your inbox with the details you need to price a job.

The sensible setup is both. Facebook and Instagram keep you visible to people who already know you. Your website is what catches the ones who do not. If you want the social side handled as well, our social media management is $250 a month.

What it costs

Our web design pricing is fixed and GST inclusive, so the GST is already inside the figure rather than added at the end. The Starter site is $750 as a one-off. The Business site is $1,500 one-off. Larger builds are quoted individually. Which tier suits your trade depends on how much there is to explain, so send us an outline and we will confirm the tier and what it covers before you pay anything.

Hosting is billed separately, at either $25 a month unmanaged or $50 a month managed. See hosting for the difference between the two. There are no lock-in contracts.

You can pay by PayID or bank transfer, Skrill, PayPal or Payoneer. Every client gets a portal where you can view and pay invoices, track how the project is going and message us directly, so you are not chasing anyone for an update.

Working with us

OptiTide is Michael James Long trading as OptiTide, ABN 38 163 865 712. Australian owned and operated, working with clients right across the country remotely.

We work to Perth time: Monday to Friday, 9 AM to 9 PM AWST. If your job is in WA, that means you are dealing with someone on your own clock rather than waiting on the east coast to wake up. The late finish is deliberate too. Nine at night AWST is eleven on the east coast, so feedback you send once you have knocked off can be picked up that evening and be waiting for you to review the next morning.

If you want to talk through what your trade needs, get in touch. You can also read how we work, browse the rest of our services, or have a look at the blog.

faqs.q: How much does a website for tradies cost?
faqs.a: The Starter site is $750 as a one-off and the Business site is $1,500 one-off, both GST inclusive. Larger or more complex builds are quoted individually. Hosting is billed separately at $25 a month unmanaged or $50 a month managed. There are no lock-in contracts, and we confirm which tier fits and what it covers before you pay anything. You can pay by PayID or bank transfer, Skrill, PayPal or Payoneer.

faqs.q: Will my site work properly on a phone?
faqs.a: The phone view is the one that matters, because someone with a broken hot water system is searching on a phone rather than sitting at a desk. What that means in practice is a tappable number near the top, a short quote form, and images light enough to load on mobile data. We go through those decisions with you and confirm what is in scope for your tier before you pay anything.

faqs.q: Do I still need a website if I already have a Facebook page?
faqs.a: They do different jobs. A Facebook page is not built to be found through a Google search the way a website is, and posts move down the feed as newer ones arrive, so older work ends up where nobody scrolls. A website is what catches someone who has never heard of you, and it gives you a quote form that lands in your inbox with the details you need to price a job. Running both is the sensible setup, and our social media management is $250 a month if you want that side handled.

faqs.q: Can you help me show up when people search for my trade in my area?
faqs.a: There are two parts to it. The build is where the structure sits: which pages you have, what each one is about, and how clearly it says where you work. We will confirm what is in scope for your tier before you pay. Improving rankings from there is ongoing work, and that is our SEO service, at $750, $1,500 or $2,500 a month. Setting up a free Google Business Profile is worth doing either way and costs you nothing.

No. The business is Australian owned and operated and registered in Victoria, and all the work is done remotely, so we have clients right across the country and there is no office to visit anywhere. What we do keep is Perth time: our hours are Monday to Friday, 9 AM to 9 PM AWST. If your job is in WA that means you are dealing with someone on your own clock, and something you send late in the WA day can still be picked up that evening. If you are on the east coast, 9 PM AWST is 11 PM your time, so feedback you send after your workday can be looked at that evening and the result reviewed the next morning.
